sentences.text: There is no question in my mind it was, because Jewish people that play with Lechia.
Even older spectators, the public, what did it come to watch, and they wanted Lechia to win.
I watched the game, because I didn't belong to that team.
The end of the game, when Maccabi won, they started to go into dressing room, and they start the-- kick mostly the public-- the public what they wanted Lechia to win.
And they start to fight with the people.
There were a lot of people from lodge that come to participate the game.
As a matter of fact, I remember one took out a revolver and shot in the air.
Everybody, they tried to get out as fast as possible, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because no matter what it is, somebody that got hurt, and they tried to hurt as many as they could.
I did belong to Ha-No'ar ha-Ziyyoni.
We had some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all kind of activities like from-- I remember we went in 1937, we went to a camp for four weeks with that organization.
And then in 1938, we went again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we spend there-- there come some people in that time from Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was very interesting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody from the young people, they knew Palestine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it was, if I'm remembering correctly, that wasn't my intention, because from my brother's first marriage, all the brothers, they went to Palestine, even the Polacks.
They said your place is not here.
I had the picture from that little girl.
She was six years old when-- when-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when they took her away with the mother.
That was when they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] liquidate the ghetto in 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The whole ghetto was liquidated in 1942 or 194-- that was already the little ghetto was liquidate in 1942 or 1943, something like this.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I know, but I just now it's--
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is so hard to describe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a picture there-- there's one, Nisek Wertajm, like I said, he was knowing-- everybody did like him.
There is not a person in the world, even being in camp.
He was a person that everybody did like him.
He was a real human being.
If he could help somebody, he did.

He started to learn tailoring in our place and when we were in the first camp, and we didn't know anything about-- everybody try to take some money with them, hide some money, and take it to the camp.
And it happens, he did talk and came over in camp and he said-- I remember he said, I borrow you $10.
[INAUDIBLE] is whatever the-- if you're going to live, you're going to-- and it happens after the war, he didn't survive.
After the war-- he got married during the war, and we gave back to his wife the $10.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] Sometime it come collapse in play.
He was like a representative from everybody.
He was a very good player, soccer.
So he did play even in the Polish teams in the combination from Tomaszow-- all Tomaszow team against the other one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have some very, very, very good friend.
As a matter of fact, next week we have a wedding in New York.
I'm going to a friend's wedding.
There were Jewish kids in this school.
I think it was Jewish kids.
I don't recall what it was set up, but mostly Jewish kids.
Not as far as I remember.
When I start tailoring, I-- I start when I was 12 years old.
No, I still go to-- went to school when came and I did tailoring.
That time is my brother took it over.
We had already expand-- make it bigger.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everybody had to teach them by themself.
It was in the what it called the [INAUDIBLE].
My brother, he got married, and he moved the shop to his place.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] What can I say?
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can't say anything.

The only thing I remember, we were-- were in a play at that time, and they said that Hitler-- 1933 when [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Kaiser Wilhelm or whatever it was, that he became Chancellor, that's all.
But the rest, nobody did believe that something like this could ever happen.
What he'd done to the Jewish population.
In 1933, because in that time, not right in 1933, a few years later, he's sent out Jewish people from Germany.
But they did come from Poland.
So a lot of people did come to Poland from Germany-- Jewish people at that time-- 1937, '38.
I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a family that come over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was in Germany before the war, and he married to a German woman.
And there-- and they have two children, a daughter and a son.
And I think in 1937 or in '38, they was-- they sent him out-- they had family in Tomaszow.
And then his wife-- she was German, real German-- and she did come to Tomaszow too.
She didn't want to leave them alone.
And then when they liquidate the ghetto and everything, she marched together when we were in the transport by train-- till the train, she marched.
They-- they-- they-- they sent out all the Jews for they were not born in Germany.
Julius, I even saw a picture.
He had a bar mitzvah in Germany-- I remember correctly-- with the tallit and everything.
He said that they tried-- they didn't talk very much, but they just-- they had to leave.
And the sister went back to Germany as a Gentile.
And it happens after the war, I was in Berlin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an Oranienburger Strasse, there was the Jewish Congress.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And she did cry.
The only thing we knew what's going on in this, but we could never ever believe that something like this happening.
The only thing we knew, Hitler.
That he is a very, very bad man.
Maybe some older people or what, they knew more than I do.
The only thing I remember was a boxing game in Hamburg, and there was a Jewish boxer from Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was in 19-- I think in 1937 or '38.
The Polish team went to Hamburg, and somehow they shout the team Hamburg [INAUDIBLE] and he did see it the way they knocked out the windows in Hamburg from Jewish stores and so on.
And when they went on the ring, and he said I'm going to teach you now, to that German what he did box against him.
And he did gave-- he knocked him out, with blood.
He said, you're going to pay with blood.
And in that time, the Polish paper-- the newspaper, they said that he didn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] express himself nice on the ring.
That the-- that Rotholz, the Jewish boxer, had not expressed himself.
That's the Polish newspaper they would say I think like this.
After all, this is sport, but he and-- the coach, the Polish coach, he took them away on the side and went back to the hotel, not to see so much.
I could hear, but I didn't pay very much attention.
That it was going on, the-- the killing, they're [SIGHS] abusing Jewish people in Germany.
As a young boy, nobody ever did-- I wouldn't say nobody, but I didn't pay very much attention.
I remember I had an account, a bank account at it called [INAUDIBLE] Polish-- in the post office there.
And near the war, I went up and wanted-- we had to stay in the line, and a lot of people there, they want to withdraw some money.
And there was an organization, a Polish youth organization and it call OZON, O-Z-O-N, OZON.
This is youth follow, like I would say, like probably like the Hitlerjugend.
And they would stay in the line and will have the Jewish people want to go near the window to draw some money, they special blocked it.
I went once in the morning, right in the morning, and withdraw.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember we went in the old country, everybody like a tailor, like me, had to go three years in the school at night time in order to finish as a, you know, if I want to open a shop, I have to finish a school.
After the school, you're going out, you could see Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] young people.
They'd stay with paint with brushes, going to brush your-- with paint up to-- that was very common.
I remember it wasn't-- I was in bed still asleep, and all of a sudden, I heard something.
And we got up and said maybe it's raining or it start thunderstorm and that's all.
And then not very long they said it's a fire not very far where we used to live.
There were two bombs right there that-- and a big-- what they did make materials, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they bombed there.
That was the first bombs there.
It was about 5:00 in the morning.
And the name of that firm was Markowic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They made good materials.
In that time when it has started bomb, and the third day, everybody started to escape, to go.
And on the way to Warsaw, that calls a small town, Rawa, that was a slaughter house at that time.
And the fourth day, say I'm going to Warsaw too.
I took my bicycle and ride to Warsaw.
And in Warsaw we had-- I didn't know where to sleep, where to go.
We didn't have anything to eat.
But somehow we got some help from-- and I slept, I remember, in a very small little synagogue-- very small.
